title: Project Miner Lord Design Documents
text:
Project Miner Lord is a medieval macro-management and political-societal simulation.
This documentation site is organized around two core systems.

DSMAS

the economic and social simulation layer
explains how goods are produced, supplied, acquired, used, stored, and carried across time
explains how seasonality, climate, stock management, and long-run social reproduction shape the realm

Politics

the political and institutional layer
explains how power is distributed, how administration works, how institutions shape opportunity, and why formal law does not always match actual practice


The goal of this documentation is not only to describe isolated mechanics, but to explain how the game is meant to feel.
The player does not manage each household one by one.
Instead, the player governs provinces, shapes institutions, plans roads, designs zoning layouts, organizes trade connections, sets warehouse priorities, influences local specialization, and observes how those decisions gradually transform the visible world.


Project Miner Lord is designed as a game where economic and political change should be visible and interpretable.
The player should experience change through:

building appearance
visible differences in NPC clothing, equipment, and transport
changing trade intensity
shortage pressure
estate rise and decline
zoning and land-value pattern
demographic change over time

In short, this is a game about ruling a realm whose economy, society, and built environment gradually change in visible ways.








The Dynamic Sequential Market Adjustment Simulation (DSMAS) is the background system that governs:

production
supply
acquisition
use
stock carry-over
shortage
fertility
next-generation human capital
occupational mobility
productive investment

It is designed around sequential adjustment rather than instant perfect balance.
Go to DSMAS



The Politics system is the higher-order structure that governs:

distribution of power
administration
institutions
legitimacy
actual practice versus formal rule
political constraint on economy and society

It is the main governing layer through which the player shapes the realm.
Go to Politics




These two systems are meant to work together.

Politics determines who can decide, who can enforce, who can access, and who can obstruct.
DSMAS determines how goods, stock, survival pressure, and long-run social change unfold under those political constraints.

In other words:

Politics shapes the rules, permissions, and frictions.
DSMAS simulates what happens inside that structure over time.




A good reading order is:

DSMAS/index.qmd
DSMAS/core-system.qmd
DSMAS/taxonomy.qmd
DSMAS/procedure.qmd
DSMAS/stock-and-seasonality.qmd
Politics/index.qmd
Politics/overview.qmd
Politics/distribution-of-power.qmd
Politics/administration.qmd
Politics/institutions.qmd
Politics/legitimacy-and-custom.qmd




This documentation emphasizes three priorities:

depth without tedious micromanagement
readable cause and effect
visible long-run transformation of the realm

The purpose of the system is not to hide complexity behind spreadsheets.
The purpose is to create a world where governance, storage, trade, climate, institutions, and social reproduction all matter in ways the player can actually see.

title: Project Miner Lord Design Documents
text:
This documentation emphasizes three priorities:

depth without tedious micromanagement
readable cause and effect
visible long-run transformation of the realm

The purpose of the system is not to hide complexity behind spreadsheets.
The purpose is to create a world where governance, storage, trade, climate, institutions, and social reproduction all matter in ways the player can actually see.

title: Stock and Season
text:
In DSMAS, stock management is a central gameplay experience. The player should care deeply about whether a province has enough food, fuel, materials, and reserve stock before winter, war, transport disruption, or local instability.
The system does not rely mainly on universal depreciation to make stock relevant. Instead, stock matters because of concrete constraints and timing asymmetry.



A core design choice is to separate Acquisition from Use. People may acquire goods now and use them later. This distinction is essential for:

harvest-season stockpiling
winter fuel preparation
food reserve management
province-level survival gameplay

Without this distinction, there is no meaningful seasonal survival logic.



DSMAS distinguishes between:

market stock
warehouse reserve
household stock

Goods in one form do not automatically become usable in another form. A province may be rich in warehouse stock and still expose some estates to shortage if access is weak.





Stock does not solve everything because storage is finite. A province or estate may want more reserve but still fail to hold it due to limited storage capacity.



The existence of stock does not guarantee practical access. A province may hold reserve but some estates may not reach it. Stock may also be locked behind administrative priority or distribution bottlenecks.



Reserve building is useful, but it ties up resources. A province that stores too much may become safer but less flexible. This creates a tradeoff between:

security
liquidity
expansion
trade responsiveness





Seasonality is one of the central survival mechanisms of the system. Winter should raise demand for:

food
fuel
housing service
warm clothing

This is not cosmetic. Seasonal pressure is one of the reasons stock, preparation, and local planning matter.



Each province has a Climate Zone and a Seasonal State. These modify both production and use, especially:

subsistence need
fuel need
housing pressure
agricultural opportunity
target stock
winter vulnerability

A simple representation of seasonal subsistence need is:
[ {c}{g,t,p} = {c}{g}^{0} m_g(p,{p,t}) ]
This means that baseline need for a good (g) rises or falls depending on where the province is and what season it currently faces.



The player must be able to directly influence stock behavior through rules such as:

priority rule
minimum stock rule
warehouse reserve target
protected local reserve

These are core controls rather than minor convenience options.



The strategic challenge is not just producing more. It is producing enough at the right time, moving goods into the right location, preserving reserve, and ensuring access under seasonal stress.
That is why DSMAS treats stock as a structured problem of:

timing
storage
access
reserve priority
local vulnerability
transport reliability

The point of the system is that time is uneven, climate is uneven, and survival depends on preparing for that unevenness.

title: DSMAS
text:
DSMAS is a medieval macro-management and political-societal simulation. The player does not control households one by one. Instead, the player governs provinces, shapes local administration, plans roads, organizes trade connections, sets warehouse priorities, induces specialization, and observes how those decisions gradually transform the visible world. The intended experience is not to stare at abstract numbers alone, but to read economic and social change through buildings, trade intensity, shortage pressure, estate rise and decline, zoning patterns, and demographic change.



DSMAS is designed to simulate the macroeconomy of a medieval world in an immersive and internally consistent way. It does not solve the whole economy in one instant equilibrium. Instead, it simulates gradual adjustment through production, supply, acquisition, use, stock accumulation, shortage, fertility, child development, social mobility, and investment.
The core design principle is simple:

people and estates react to what recently happened
goods are not always available where they are needed
information is limited
transport takes time
storage is finite
access to stock is unequal
social change is gradual
the next generation matters




DSMAS operates mainly at the level of the Province. The Province is the main economic, administrative, and visual unit through which the player experiences the system. Markets are organized mainly at the province level, but provinces are not fully connected from the start. Trade depends on available stock, merchant capacity, route access, transport time, transport cost, and known market information.
The player is expected to intervene through:

road network planning
zoning and land-use planning
warehouse minimum stock settings
storage priority settings
trade network designation
regional policy selection
specialization induction
guard and mercenary deployment




DSMAS is a sequential market-adjustment simulation. The world changes through ordered procedures rather than instant perfect balance. This is intentional.
Goods may exist but still fail to arrive where they are needed. Provinces may hold stock but lack practical access. Information may be incomplete. Transport may be slow or unsafe. Storage may be insufficient. Working capital may be locked into reserve. These are not accidents. They are part of the logic of the system.



The player should be able to broadly understand why something happened. A shortage, stagnation, overflowed warehouse, winter crisis, or estate decline should not feel arbitrary. The system should expose cause and effect through visible signals, not only through hidden equations.
In practical terms, DSMAS tries to make the economy legible through:

map overlays
market panels
zoning views
land-value gradients
estate summaries
trade movement
shortage warnings
winter pressure indicators




This documentation is split into four main parts:

Core System
Taxonomy
Procedure
Stock and Season

Each page covers one layer of the overall simulation architecture.

title: DSMAS
text:
This documentation is split into four main parts:

Core System
Taxonomy
Procedure
Stock and Season

Each page covers one layer of the overall simulation architecture.

title: Core System
text:
DSMAS is a sequential market-adjustment simulation. This means the economy does not jump to a single perfect equilibrium in one step. Instead, the world changes through ordered procedures in which production, supply, acquisition, use, and long-run transition occur in sequence.
This structure matters for both realism and gameplay clarity. The player should be able to distinguish whether a problem came from weak production, poor supply allocation, weak stock access, excessive acquisition by some groups, shortage at the point of use, or weak long-run development.



DSMAS should feel deep, but not tedious. The player should shape the direction of society rather than manually manage every tiny transaction. For this reason, the system uses:

estate-level aggregation
province-level management
stock rules instead of constant hand-tuning
visible signals instead of hidden complexity




Economic development should appear in the world itself. A richer province should look different from a poorer one. A winter-stressed province should feel different from a stable one. A commercial province should feel different from a stagnant one.
Social and economic change should be legible through:

buildings
markets
trade movement
clothing and transport
visible estate composition
local prosperity or decline






The Province is the main governable unit. It is the place where the player reads the world, plans infrastructure, organizes storage, sees local shortage, and interprets visible development.



The Estate is the main aggregated decision-making unit. DSMAS does not simulate every individual separately. Instead, it works with Social Estates so that the system remains interpretable and computationally manageable.



The Cohort is the main intergenerational unit. Long-run human capital formation and social reproduction are carried through child cohorts and later replacement into adult social structure.




Most important outcomes in DSMAS are endogenous. Economic outcomes and broader social change emerge from the interaction of production, distribution, acquisition, reserve management, fertility, child investment, mobility, and investment.
The system is meant to determine:

production
supply
acquisition
use
stock carry-over
shortage
fertility
next-generation human capital
occupational mobility
promotion and demotion
productive investment




Not everything is endogenous. Some objects remain fixed background structure. These include:

production parameters
preference parameters
storability class
transport geography
baseline institutional rules
selected administrative capacity constraints
selected environmental constraints
climate generation rules




Politics is the higher-order constraint structure. DSMAS is the background simulation of exchange, stock management, and social reproduction. Political decisions modify enforcement, allocation, access, security, and adjustment speed.
In that sense, politics does not replace DSMAS. It sits above it and changes how strongly the underlying economic system can function.

title: Procedure
text:
DSMAS works through ordered procedures. It does not assume that everything happens at once. This is important for both realism and gameplay clarity.
The full system is divided into three procedural layers:

Exchange Clock Procedure
Estate Clock Procedure
Generational Clock Procedure



The Exchange Clock handles short-run material flow. It turns plans into actual material outcomes.


Production Tick is where productive plans become realized output. Production is distinct from movement and distinct from use. Seasonal production matters especially for agriculture.
Inputs:

planned production vector
available labor
available tangible stock
productive asset position
capacity utilization plan
institutional access
local disruption
Seasonal State
Climate Zone

Outputs:

realized production
used input stock
unused capacity
production shortfall
newly available goods




Supply Tick determines where newly available goods are placed before final acquisition and use. Goods are divided among local market stock, warehouse reserve, outbound shipment, and protected minimum stock.
Inputs:

realized production
existing stock
warehouse condition
planned local supply
planned outbound supply
merchant capacity
route access
priority rule
minimum stock rule
administrative instruction

Outputs:

local market stock
outbound shipment plan
warehouse reserve assignment
protected minimum stock
unallocated goods
realized outbound delivery
delivery failure
route congestion




Acquisition Tick is where estates attempt to obtain goods that have actually become available. This is not the same as final use. Estates may acquire goods for immediate use, reserve holding, winter preparation, or later household consumption.
Inputs:

accessible local market stock
household stock
subsistence allocation
reserve allocation
target stock
expected subsistence pressure
expected winter pressure
expected availability pressure
spending capacity
access constraint
storage capacity

Outputs:

realized acquisition
household stock inflow
failed acquisition
acquisition shortfall
stock pressure after acquisition




Use Tick is where estates and local users actually consume or apply goods that are already accessible to them. Goods may be bought now and used later, so acquisition and use must remain distinct.
Inputs:

household stock
realized acquisition
subsistence need
discretionary allocation
Seasonal State
Climate Zone
housing pressure
fuel need

Outputs:

realized subsistence use
realized discretionary use
fuel use
housing-service use
unmet subsistence need
unmet discretionary need
post-use stock




Exchange Update Tick records what happened in the short run and prepares signals for later decisions.
Inputs:

realized production
realized outbound delivery
realized acquisition
realized use
delivery failure
unmet need
remaining stock
unsold stock
route congestion
warehouse stress

Outputs:

shortage signal
surplus signal
delivery instability signal
exchange success signal
updated carry-over stock
signal memory for later expectation formation





The Estate Clock handles estate-level planning and social allocation.


Estates first review recent conditions so that decision-making is grounded in experienced reality.
Inputs:

shortage memory
delivery success memory
stock pressure
household stock
known route access
estate spending capacity
local security
administrative reliability
Seasonal State
Climate Zone

Output:

estate decision-state summary




Expectation Formation generates simple forecasts based on recent local experience and known access. It is not perfect foresight. It is a practical estimate.
Inputs:

lagged shortage frequency
lagged delivery success
lagged stock pressure
known route access
transport reliability
administrative reliability
estate spending pressure
recent social stress
Seasonal State
Climate Zone
time until next major replenishment

Outputs:

expected production viability
expected trade opportunity
expected subsistence pressure
expected availability pressure
expected winter pressure
expected child-investment return
expected mobility opportunity
target stock




Consumption, fertility, child investment, reserve building, and productive investment should be treated as one broad allocation problem.
The conceptual resource identity is
[ Y_t^{e} = C_t^{subs,e} + C_t^{disc,e} + F_t^{e} + I_t^{child,e} + I_t^{prod,e} + R_t^{e} ]
where:

(C_t^{subs,e}): subsistence allocation
(C_t^{disc,e}): discretionary allocation
(F_t^{e}): fertility-related expenditure
(I_t^{child,e}): next-generation human capital investment
(I_t^{prod,e}): productive investment
(R_t^{e}): reserve allocation

Inputs:

estate spending capacity
expected subsistence pressure
expected availability pressure
expected winter pressure
expected child-investment return
expected mobility opportunity
institutional constraint
demographic composition
storage capacity
working-capital pressure

Outputs:

subsistence allocation
discretionary allocation
fertility allocation
child investment allocation
productive investment allocation
reserve allocation




Estates decide whether to commit resources to future production, building, capacity growth, or local improvement. The commitment is immediate, but realization is delayed.
Inputs:

productive investment allocation
expected production viability
road condition
construction constraint
institutional permission
security condition
local specialization profile
working-capital pressure

Outputs:

committed productive project
committed capital expansion
committed construction demand
future capacity pipeline




Some choices prepare long-run transformation without changing society instantly.
Inputs:

fertility allocation
child investment allocation
child cohort structure
institutional access
estate barrier
estate privilege

Outputs:

next-generation human capital pipeline
future cohort quality indicator
future mobility eligibility indicator
demographic transition queue





The Generational Clock handles slow social transformation.


Long-run human capital formation is modeled mainly through children.
A conceptual form is
[ h_{t+1}^{child,e} = H(, s_t, a_t, _t) ]
where:

(n_t^{e}): child cohort size
(s_t): subsistence and health condition
(a_t): access condition
(_t): institutional inclusion

Inputs:

accumulated child investment
child cohort size
nutrition condition
living-standard condition
institutional access
estate barrier
local stability

Output:

realized child human capital




Children later become the next adult layer of society.
Inputs:

realized child human capital
cohort transition queue
estate entry rule
institutional access
local opportunity structure

Outputs:

new adult cohort entry
updated estate composition
replacement pressure by estate




Long-run mobility should not be decided instantly in one short-run window.
Inputs:

mobility eligibility indicator
new cohort quality
estate barrier
estate privilege
institutional openness
local demand for roles

Outputs:

promotion realization
demotion realization
occupational transition realization
updated mobility history




Institutions matter most when they shape repeated access across generations.
Inputs:

institutional reach
administrative continuity
educational access
political inclusion
security stability

Outputs:

updated institutional access profile
updated inclusion pattern
updated long-run barrier structure




Long-run development should emerge from repeated accumulated change.
Inputs:

updated estate composition
updated human capital profile
durable capital stock
road quality
trade-network depth
institutional reach
provincial stress history

Outputs:

updated long-run provincial development
updated specialization profile
updated long-run social structure

Seasonal State
Climate Zone

Output:

estate decision-state summary




Expectation Formation generates simple forecasts based on recent local experience and known access. It is not perfect foresight. It is a practical estimate.
Inputs:

lagged shortage frequency
lagged delivery success
lagged stock pressure
known route access
transport reliability
administrative reliability
estate spending pressure
recent social stress
Seasonal State
Climate Zone
time until next major replenishment

Outputs:

expected production viability
expected trade opportunity
expected subsistence pressure
expected availability pressure
expected winter pressure
expected child-investment return
expected mobility opportunity
target stock




Consumption, fertility, child investment, reserve building, and productive investment should be treated as one broad allocation problem.
The conceptual resource identity is
[ Y_t^{e} = C_t^{subs,e} + C_t^{disc,e} + F_t^{e} + I_t^{child,e} + I_t^{prod,e} + R_t^{e} ]
where:

(C_t^{subs,e}): subsistence allocation
(C_t^{disc,e}): discretionary allocation
(F_t^{e}): fertility-related expenditure
(I_t^{child,e}): next-generation human capital investment
(I_t^{prod,e}): productive investment
(R_t^{e}): reserve allocation

Inputs:

estate spending capacity
expected subsistence pressure
expected availability pressure
expected winter pressure
expected child-investment return
expected mobility opportunity
institutional constraint
demographic composition
storage capacity
working-capital pressure

Outputs:

subsistence allocation
discretionary allocation
fertility allocation
child investment allocation
productive investment allocation
reserve allocation




Estates decide whether to commit resources to future production, building, capacity growth, or local improvement. The commitment is immediate, but realization is delayed.
Inputs:

productive investment allocation
expected production viability
road condition
construction constraint
institutional permission
security condition
local specialization profile
working-capital pressure

Outputs:

committed productive project
committed capital expansion
committed construction demand
future capacity pipeline




Some choices prepare long-run transformation without changing society instantly.
Inputs:

fertility allocation
child investment allocation
child cohort structure
institutional access
estate barrier
estate privilege

Outputs:

next-generation human capital pipeline
future cohort quality indicator
future mobility eligibility indicator
demographic transition queue

title: Procedure
text:
The Generational Clock handles slow social transformation.


Long-run human capital formation is modeled mainly through children.
A conceptual form is
[ h_{t+1}^{child,e} = H(, s_t, a_t, _t) ]
where:

(n_t^{e}): child cohort size
(s_t): subsistence and health condition
(a_t): access condition
(_t): institutional inclusion

Inputs:

accumulated child investment
child cohort size
nutrition condition
living-standard condition
institutional access
estate barrier
local stability

Output:

realized child human capital




Children later become the next adult layer of society.
Inputs:

realized child human capital
cohort transition queue
estate entry rule
institutional access
local opportunity structure

Outputs:

new adult cohort entry
updated estate composition
replacement pressure by estate




Long-run mobility should not be decided instantly in one short-run window.
Inputs:

mobility eligibility indicator
new cohort quality
estate barrier
estate privilege
institutional openness
local demand for roles

Outputs:

promotion realization
demotion realization
occupational transition realization
updated mobility history




Institutions matter most when they shape repeated access across generations.
Inputs:

institutional reach
administrative continuity
educational access
political inclusion
security stability

Outputs:

updated institutional access profile
updated inclusion pattern
updated long-run barrier structure




Long-run development should emerge from repeated accumulated change.
Inputs:

updated estate composition
updated human capital profile
durable capital stock
road quality
trade-network depth
institutional reach
provincial stress history

Outputs:

updated long-run provincial development
updated specialization profile
updated long-run social structure

title: Taxonomy
text:
DSMAS does not use one single time step for everything. Different parts of the world move at different speeds. Goods can be produced, moved, acquired, and used relatively quickly. Estate-level planning changes more slowly. Child development, cohort replacement, and long-run social transformation change more slowly still.
To keep the system intuitive and computationally manageable, time is divided into three layers:

Exchange Clock
Estate Clock
Generational Clock

In addition, the world has a continuously changing Seasonal State and Climate Context. These are not separate full clocks. They are environmental conditions that modify demand, production opportunity, access pressure, and stock targets.



The Exchange Clock handles short-run material movement and local adjustment. It is the fastest layer in DSMAS. This is where goods are produced, allocated, shipped, acquired, used, and carried over.
Its five components are:

Production Tick
Supply Tick
Acquisition Tick
Use Tick
Exchange Update Tick




The Estate Clock handles estate-level planning and social allocation. It is slower than the Exchange Clock. This is the layer where estates review recent conditions, form expectations, allocate resources, commit investment, and prepare long-run transition.
Its five components are:

State Review
Expectation Formation
Joint Estate Allocation
Investment Commitment
Transition Preparation




The Generational Clock handles the slowest form of change. It governs intergenerational development and long-run social transformation.
Its five components are:

Child Human Capital Realization
Cohort Replacement
Estate Mobility Realization
Institutional Transmission
Slow Developmental Update




Each province has a Climate Zone and a current Seasonal State. These affect both production and use. In particular, they affect:

subsistence need
fuel need
housing pressure
agricultural opportunity
target stock
winter vulnerability

The basic design principle is that production can be seasonal, demand can also be seasonal, and stock exists because time is uneven.



The set of geographic units is
[ G = {p, r, s, c} ]
where:

(p): Province
(r): Region
(s): Subcontinent
(c): Continent

The belonging location of an estate or population block can be written as
[ G_0 = {p_0, r_0, s_0, c_0} ]
In gameplay terms, the Province is the most important unit.



Population is not mainly simulated one individual at a time. Instead, decision-making is aggregated by Social Estate, denoted by (e).
The main estates are:

The Destitute
The Commoners
The Artisans
The Merchants
The Clergy
The Soldiers
The Virtuosos
The Nomads

This aggregation is used because it is more intuitive for the player, easier to connect to politics and institutions, and far cheaper to compute than full agent-level simulation.





These are mainly updated through the Exchange Clock:

local stock
market stock
household stock
merchant allocation
realized delivery
realized acquisition
realized use
shortage
unsold stock
route disruption
warehouse stress




These are mainly updated through the Estate Clock:

estate spending capacity
subsistence pressure
fertility pressure
child investment allocation
production plan
supply plan
reserve allocation
productive investment commitment
local administrative performance
target stock
priority rule




These are mainly updated through the Generational Clock:

next-generation human capital
estate composition
durable capital stock
road quality
trade-network depth
institutional reach
long-run provincial development
cohort transition structure







Tangible goods can be stored and carried over. Typical examples include:

food
fuel
material inputs
construction goods
household durables
trade goods
warm clothing




Non-tangible goods cannot be stored in the same way and are used within the relevant decision window. Typical examples include:

education access
artistic service
administrative service
religious service
prestige consumption
protection-related service
housing service
